retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for these patients is guarded and depends on the severity of associated anomalies. [11] [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Sturge-Weber Syndrome -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of Sturge-Weber syndrome is best accomplished with an interprofessional team approach. The team may include a geneticist, pediatricians, pediatric ophthalmologists, pediatric radiologists, pediatric neurologists, nurse practitioners, and pediatric neurosurgeons. There is no cure for the syndrome and all treatments are symptom-based. In addition to preventing seizures, patients need ophthalmology follow-up due to the risk of developing glaucoma. The skin lesions can be managed by a dermatologist. Nurses play an important role in patient and parent education.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Brain Tumors -- Differential Diagnosis -- Neurocutaneous Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: Neurofibromatosis (NF), tuberous sclerosis, Sturge-Weber syndrome, Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) syndrome, and hereditary telangiectasia are well-known syndromes with central nervous system (CNS) manifestations that are non-neoplastic. These syndromes are known to predispose to specific types of brain tumors. For example, NF-1 is associated with low-grade gliomas. Tuberous sclerosis may be associated with SEGA (subependymal giant cell astrocytomas), and hemangioblastomas are seen in VHL. Physicians may find it challenging to differentiate between the non-neoplastic NF-related myelin vacuolation spots and low-grade glioma in settings of NF-1. Cortical and subcortical T2 hyperintense tubers in tuberous sclerosis may mimic gliomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Osler-Weber-Rendu Disease (Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Most HHT patients who have adequate access to healthcare will have normal life expectancies. There is a bimodal distribution of mortality, with peaks at age 50 and then from 60 to 79. Most of the mortality of HHT is the result of complications of AVMs, particularly in the brain, lungs, and GI system.

retrieved_docs.title: Sturge-Weber Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The patient and parents should be made to understand that there is no cure for this disease and the maximum we can offer is to give symptomatic treatment and control the neurologic and ocular manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: Sturge-Weber Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of Sturge-Weber syndrome is not well known and estimated to be 1 in 20,000-50,000 live births. SWS affects males and females equally and there is no race predilection. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Sturge-Weber Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A seizure is usually the first neurological manifestation of Sturge-Weber syndrome. Infantile spasms are seen in approximately 90% of affected patients in the first year of life followed by atonic, tonic, or myoclonic seizures. Increasing hemiparesis commonly accompanies seizures. Seizures progressively become refractory to medication. [3] Facial nevus (port-wine stain) is another common finding and is typically seen along the ophthalmic or maxillary segment of the trigeminal nerve (forehead, cheeks). It is typically unilateral, present at birth, and does not change with the age of the patient. [4] This appearance is in sharp contrast to the infantile hemangioma which is more common than a port-wine stain; however, it is not present at birth. It slowly grows and is followed by involution. A child with a facial port-wine stain has a 10% to 35% risk of brain involvement. If there is involvement of both upper and lower eyelids, then the risk of glaucoma increases up to 50%. Glaucoma is almost always ipsilateral to the facial port-wine stain. Not all patients with port-wine stains have Sturge-Weber syndrome. Patients with Sturge-Weber syndrome may present with cerebral symptoms without facial findings. Ocular involvement in infancy may present with increased vascularity of the conjunctiva, eye enlargement, strabismus, and increased tearing. Other symptoms are intellectual disability, early handedness, and gaze preferences. Diffuse choroidal hemangioma is seen in about 20% of patients with Sturge-Weber syndrome and is usually on the same side as a facial port-wine stain. [5] Choroidal hemangiomas grow slowly and usually do not cause any symptoms. Retina overlying the choroidal hemangioma may be normal; however, it may also show pathological changes such as epithelial atrophy/proliferation, drusen formation, or detachment.

Recognizing that intracranial hemorrhage is a subset of neonatal hemorrhage helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and treatment on the central nervous system when newborn bleeding is suspected.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all identified as specific manifestations or related entities of مرض السكري (diabetes). In clinical practice, مرض السكري is linked not only to its primary metabolic disturbances but also to other pancreatic disorders that are distinct from diabetes itself, highlighting the broader spectrum of pancreatic pathology that may coexist with or mimic diabetic presentations. Additionally, مرض السكري is associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder), reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy and other vision‑related complications in patients with diabetes. Furthermore, مرض السكري is recognized as having a child condition, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome), underscoring the interrelationship between diabetes and the cluster of metabolic risk factors that include obesity, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and insulin resistance. Together, these connections emphasize the heterogeneous nature of diabetes, its potential neonatal and developmental presentations, its association with other pancreatic and ocular disorders, and its role as a central component of metabolic syndrome, all of which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
retrieved_docs.content: with or lead to infectious complications around birth. Overall, the knowledge network portrays neonatal disorders as an interconnected group of conditions that include traumatic cerebral injury, intracranial and neonatal hemorrhage, perinatal infections, metabolic diseases such as transient neonatal diabetes, neurologic problems like developmental delay and epilepsy, and extreme prematurity. Birth trauma is a key antecedent of intracranial bleeding, which is classified under neonatal hemorrhage, while perinatal infection serves both as a parent condition for unspecified perinatal disorders and as a member of the broader neonatal disorder set. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns presenting with any of these interrelated pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the broader condition مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). These specific neonatal and developmental presentations are therefore recognized as manifestations or subtypes of diabetes mellitus. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, indicating that retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy commonly occur in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians identify atypical early‑onset forms of diabetes and monitor for ocular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2093
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة” (neonatal disorder) is recognized as an allowable diagnostic qualifier. Within this broader neonatal disorder, two specific conditions are identified as its children: “كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية” (fetal erythroblastosis) and “رضح الولادة” (birth‑related condition). Both “كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية” and “رضح الولادة” are also listed individually as permissible diagnostic qualifiers. Consequently, clinicians consider the neonatal disorder as an overarching diagnosis that can be further specified by the presence of fetal erythroblastosis or the birth‑related condition, facilitating more precise assessment and management of newborn patients.
